title: Principal-Agent Problems

content.statement: Agents (employees, executives) may pursue their own interests when those diverge from principals (owners).
content.question: Where are my team's actual incentives pulling them away from what the company needs?
content.equation: Incentive Alignment = Goal Overlap × Measurability × Skin in the Game
content.mental_model: Renter and a landlord, different incentives, same building.
content.business_lens: Design compensation, equity, and decision rights so agent incentives match principal goals; expect drift without it.
content.life_lens: When trusting someone with your interests, ask what their incentive actually rewards.
sop: Redesign compensation, ownership, or process to align
Audit alignment annually as the company grows
content.explainer: Bonuses tied to top-line revenue produce sales reps who close bad-fit deals. CEO comp tied to short-term EPS produces buybacks instead of R&D. The principal-agent problem is structural; cultural exhortation rarely fixes what bad incentives create.
questions: Where are my team's actual incentives pulling them away from what the company needs?
Pick one person whose decisions affect your bottom line, a contractor, a partner, a manager. What does their compensation actually reward them for doing?
When has someone technically hit their target while quietly hurting the business, and did the incentive get fixed, or did you just talk to them about it?

definitions.def: When the person acting on your behalf (the agent) has incentives that diverge from your own (the principal), causing them to act in their own interest instead of yours.

content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Goal Overlap 6, Measurability 7, Skin in the Game 4. Score = (6 plus 7 plus 4) divided by 3 = 5.7. Higher means the people acting for you are structurally rewarded for doing what's actually good for the company; lower means you're relying on trust to cover for a misaligned incentive.
content.closing_statement: Incentives predict behavior better than trust does.

caption:
Principle Nº 313: Principal-Agent Problems

Agents (employees, executives) may pursue their own interests when those diverge from principals (owners). Bonuses tied to top-line revenue produce sales reps who close bad-fit deals.

Ask yourself: Where are my team's actual incentives pulling them away from what the company needs?
